session_hint: the Nginx 502 Bad Gateway error investigation
question: What did we conclude was causing the Nginx 502 Bad Gateway errors?
turns: user
Intermittent 502s from Nginx, error rate around 2%, spiky. Upstream is a Python FastAPI app running on uvicorn. No recent deployments.
user
Checked Nginx worker_processes and worker_connections — both set appropriately (auto and 1024). Workers are not maxed out at peak load. Not a concurrency limit issue.
user
Looked at proxy_buffer_size and proxy_buffers configuration — values are standard, no buffer overflow errors appearing in nginx error.log. Ruled that out.
user
Checked SSL termination at Nginx — grep'd error.log for SSL errors. Zero SSL errors in the relevant time window. Not an SSL issue.
user
Noticed 502 errors correlate strongly with requests arriving on connections that have been idle for 30-75 seconds. Connections that were recently used work fine.
user
Looked at uvicorn's keepalive settings: --timeout-keep-alive is set to 30 (the default). Uvicorn closes any idle HTTP keepalive connection after 30 seconds.
user
Checked Nginx upstream keepalive block: keepalive_timeout is set to 75 seconds. Nginx holds connections in its upstream pool for up to 75 seconds, assuming they remain valid.
user

The gap is the problem: uvicorn closes the connection at 30s, but Nginx still considers it live until 75s. Between 30s and 75s of idleness, Nginx picks the connection from its pool to serve a new request, gets a TCP RST from uvicorn (connection already closed), and returns a 502 to the client.
user

Root cause: Nginx upstream keepalive timeout (75s) exceeds FastAPI/uvicorn's keepalive timeout (30s). Nginx reuses connections that the app server has already closed, resulting in stale-connection 502 errors.
user
Fix: set keepalive_timeout in the Nginx upstream block to 25 seconds — safely below uvicorn's 30-second limit — so Nginx retires connections before uvicorn does.
user
502 rate dropped to 0% within 2 minutes of the Nginx reload. Monitored for 4 hours. Clean.
user
Worker process limits, proxy buffer settings, and SSL termination were all clean throughout. Closing the incident.
